historicalBlurb: Freddye Scarborough Henderson was a pioneering businesswoman who broke barriers as the first African American to study Fashion Merchandising at her institution. She also helped establish a national organization for Black fashion designers and organized a major fashion show for African Americans in 1953. Together with her husband, she founded Henderson Travel Service in Atlanta, the first fully accredited Black-owned travel agency in America. Henderson Travel Service specialized in cultural trips to Africa to help individuals reconnect with their heritage, and most notably arranged Dr. Martin Luther King Jr.'s historic trip to Oslo to accept the Nobel Peace Prize.

John Lewis was a fearless civil rights icon and longtime U.S. Congressman who dedicated his life to justice and equality. As chairman of the SNCC, he helped organize sit-ins and voter registration drives across the South. His courageous leadership during the brutal "Bloody Sunday" march in Selma helped pave the way for the Voting Rights Act of 1965.
imageUrlSourcing: U.S. Congress official portrait, public domain

historicalBlurb: Lillian Miles Lewis was an Atlanta-based educator and librarian whose quiet strength and steadfast support proved instrumental in her husband John Lewis's rise from city council member to U.S. Congressman. Her own contributions through her career at Clark Atlanta University left a long lasting mark on Atlanta's academic community.
historicalBlurbSourcing: John and Lillian Miles Lewis Foundation

historicalBlurb: Julian Bond was a groundbreaking civil rights leader, statesman, and educator whose tireless advocacy helped reshape American democracy. A co-founder of the Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee and a two-decade veteran of the Georgia legislature, Bond extended his fight for equality beyond race, championing LGBT rights and social justice. Bond served as chairman of the NAACP and shaped the next generation of activists as a university professor.

Jean Childs Young was a dedicated educator and civic leader who developed early childhood curriculum for Atlanta Public Schools and authored "Bridging the Gap" (1970) to help parents support their children's learning at home. She helped launch Atlanta Junior College (now Atlanta Metropolitan College), championed institutions like SciTrek and the APEX Museum, and supported educational initiatives tied to Atlanta's 1996 Olympics. As the wife of civil rights leader and Atlanta Mayor Andrew Young, her lasting impact is honored through scholarships and awards.

Graham W. Jackson Sr. was a celebrated Atlanta musician whose heartfelt accordion performance at President Franklin D. Roosevelt's funeral in 1945 became one of the most iconic images in American history, reflecting both his extraordinary talent and the cultural influence of Atlanta's Black community during the era of segregation.

Martin Luther King Sr., known as “Daddy King”, was a prominent Baptist pastor and civil rights leader whose four decades leading Atlanta's Ebenezer Baptist Church made him a cornerstone of both his community and the broader struggle for racial equality. As a fierce opponent of segregation, a leader within the NAACP, and the guiding moral force behind his son Martin Luther King Jr., his courage and conviction helped lay the foundation for one of the most transformative movements in American history.
imageUrlSourcing: White House Staff Photographer / NARA, public domain

historicalBlurb: Grace Towns Hamilton was a pioneering civil rights leader and the first Black woman elected to the Georgia General Assembly. Her tireless advocacy for education reform, social justice, and equal opportunity broke racial and gender barriers in public office and created lasting space for greater representation in Georgia politics.

John Wesley Dobbs was one of Atlanta's most influential African American leaders of the mid-20th century. His relentless pursuit of civil rights and political empowerment earned him the honorary title "Mayor of Auburn Avenue." As a founding member of the Atlanta Civic and Political League and champion of Black voter registration, he helped transform Sweet Auburn into a thriving cultural and civic hub while mobilizing over 10,000 African American voters across Georgia.

Ludie Clay Andrews was a trailblazing healthcare pioneer whose distinction as Georgia's first African American registered nurse and her enduring leadership at Spelman College's MacVicar Hospital helped open doors for generations of Black healthcare providers across the South.

Annie L. McPheeters was one of Atlanta's first African American librarians. Her career at Clark Atlanta University, and later as the first African American reference librarian at Georgia State College, was defined by her commitment to expanding literacy and library access within the Black community. Through special literacy programs and collaboration with Black advocacy groups, she played a pivotal role in ending segregation in Atlanta's public libraries.

Herman J. Russell was a self-made Atlanta entrepreneur who rose from humble beginnings to build H.J. Russell & Company into the largest minority-owned real estate and construction business in the United States. A close confidant of Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. and the first African American president of the Atlanta Chamber of Commerce, his company shaped Atlanta's modern skyline through landmark projects including Hartsfield-Jackson Airport and the 1996 Olympic Stadium.

Bazoline Estelle Usher was a pioneering Atlanta educator. Her 50-year career culminated in her appointment as Director of Negro Schools for Atlanta Public Schools, the highest position an African American had ever held in the district, and made her the first African American to hold an office at Atlanta City Hall. Bazoline also founded some of the South's earliest Girl Scout troops for African American girls, and her legacy is honored by a middle school bearing her name and her induction into the Georgia Women of Achievement hall of fame.

Alonzo Herndon was a remarkable Atlanta entrepreneur who rose from slavery to become one of the first African American millionaires in the United States. He built his wealth through a chain of elite barbershops and the founding of Atlanta Life Insurance Company in 1905. Herndon transformed himself into a symbol of Black economic achievement whose legacy endures through his nationally recognized historic home and the institution he built into one of the most successful African American businesses in the country.

William H. Crogman was an distinguished Caribbean-born educator and classicist who dedicated his career to Clark University of Atlanta. At Clark University he served as a professor of Greek and Latin and later as the school's president. Crogman was a tireless advocate for equal education and civil rights, and his lasting influence is honored through an Atlanta school and chapel bearing his name. His legacy stands as a testament to the transformative power of education in the post-Civil War South.

Reverend Robert Grant was a pioneering faith leader who founded Zion Hill Baptist Church in 1872. He organized the congregation alongside seven members who sought a place of worship closer to their homes. Drawing from his spiritual insight and genuine love of people, he established the church's foundational structure, organizing the Deacon Board, Mothers Board, and Sunday School, and setting Zion Hill on a path of remarkable early growth and spiritual vitality.
historicalBlurbSourcing: Courtesy of Zion Hill Baptist Church Archives
